# **Cost Risks of Migrating Spark from EMR to EKS**

Status: *Under review*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Last Updated: Dec 3, 2024

| Date | Updates |
| :---- | :---- |
| Dec 3, 2024 | Shared in \#team-data-infra for internal review ([thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1733246553589099)) |
| Dec 9, 2024 | Added a new potential [risk](#238%-increase-based-on-latest-fy26-road-map-which-reverse-some-key-cost-saving-efforts-on-emr) |

# Summary

This document provides an analysis of the potential cost increase associated with migrating Spark workloads from the current EMR infrastructure to EKS infrastructure. This analysis is based on today’s configurations (December 2024), so we can anticipate fast improvements on some categories. While some risks can be mitigated, others remain difficult to resolve due to fundamental differences between YARN and Kubernetes. 

The key risks of cost increase are below:

| Cost Increase Impact | Details | Comment |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **200% (or $8 million)**  annual cost increase from cross-AZ data transfer | This issue can be mitigated, but the best mitigation is to restrict the EKS cluster to one zone, adopt Apache Celeborn, carefully control autoscaling behavior and purchase ODCR to guarantee capacity. See details in [below](#$8-million-annual-cost-increase-from-cross-az-data-transfer) |  |
| **42% increase** from EC2 instance family | Transitioning from cost-effective Graviton3 instances to more expensive alternatives will increase EC2 expenses by approximately 42%. This issue can be mitigated by ensuring the EKS cluster to use the most desirable instance types (e.g. r7g.16xlarge, r8g.16xlarge and larger r8g instances) and also have ODCR guarantees on all of these instances. This also means that we need to forfeit the key advantages we aimed to leverage by using Kubernetes, Karpenter’s dynamic provisioning and autoscaling. See detail [here](#42%-cost-increase-from-ec2-instance-family-differences).  |  |
| **35% increase** from long shuffle tracking | This increase stems from the fundamental differences between YARN and Kubernetes, making it very challenging to mitigate while maintaining the reliability status quo of EMR. See details [here](#35%-cost-increase-from-shuffle-tracking-timeout) |  |
| **25% increase** caused by 500% increase for EBS cost | This increase also stems from the fundamental nature of using PVC for Spark. See details regarding this risk [here](#25%-increase-of-cluster-cost-from-significantly-increased-ebs-cost). |  |
| **150% increase** from default memory configuration | to maintain reliability status quo due to higher OOM risks on k8s. This large risk can be mitigated to an extent, but will not match the cost efficiency of EMR. See detail [below](#150%-memory-overhead-to-avoid-oom-failures). See slack [thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1733242957813589) for discussion. |  |
| **238% increase** from FY26 roadmap with multi-AZ, autoscaling, undesirable spot instances | In FY26, we will adopt spot instances, multi-AZ availability and autoscaling. This is a reverse of what we did for EMR in FY25Q1, and we can calculate an accurate estimate of the cost increase based on cost data. See details [here](#238%-increase-based-on-latest-fy26-road-map-which-reverse-some-key-cost-saving-efforts-on-emr).  |  |

# $8 million annual cost increase from cross-AZ data transfer {#$8-million-annual-cost-increase-from-cross-az-data-transfer}

There are unavailable extra data transfer costs associated with the EKS cluster compared to our EMR clusters ([AWS doc](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/addressing-latency-and-data-transfer-costs-on-eks-using-istio/#:~:text=Depending%20on%20how%20you%20have,NAT%20gateway%20data%20transfer%20charges.)). In this section, we address the most significant costs with the greatest impact.

Data shuffling is an inherent and sometimes expected aspect of Spark. On our current EMR infrastructure, there are no additional data transfer costs for shuffling because all machines operate within the same AZ. However, with the current EKS cluster for Spark (**spark-prod-iad**), it can acquire machines from all 6 possible zones in the region[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.rwe72e8dlnev).  The current running workload for the “beagle\_thresholds” dag [appears to use cross-AZ shuffling](https://slack-github.com/slack/spark-on-k8s/blob/master/SparkApps/security/beagle_thresholds_user_downloads/1_user_downloads_agg.yaml). The cross-AZ data transfer incurs a cost of $0.01 per GB in each direction, or $0.02 per GB total shuffle.  Based on shuffle-related data from the past couple of months across our clusters, we can anticipate an additional $8 million annually in cross-AZ data transfer costs for shuffling alone[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.f3ufq1gu0hc0)—expenses that do not exist in our current Spark on EMR setup.

One possible solution is to use nodeSelector to ensure that all pods of the same Spark application are launched within the same availability zone (AZ). However, the nodeSelector is hard-coded in the YAML file. Assuming AWS consistently has sufficient capacity to launch all pods in the specified AZ for each application, this approach effectively partitions the single logical EKS cluster into multiple physical clusters, one in each AZ.

Given the inherently batch-oriented nature of Spark workloads and the inherently “service-oriented” nature of typical Kubernetes workloads, and the fact that we cannot use spot instances[\[20\]](#bookmark=id.n2do5jd8cfuq) and we have a limited options for desirable EC2 instance types[\[21\]](#bookmark=id.6y17fp0y5an), it is unlikely that existing EKS infrastructure that the bedrock team maintains can absorb the bursty demands of our Spark workloads. If we were to allow spark-prod-iad to operate across all six zones, we would essentially be creating six smaller physical clusters. We anticipate significant cost increases[\[25\]](#bookmark=id.tih5liz9br3y) and heightened reliability risks[\[22\]](#bookmark=id.qippfqyr1vgz) from splitting clusters into smaller units.

Another option is to productionize Apache Celeborn in each Availability Zone (see [Improving Spark Shuffle Reliability](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1xkddnyMmSJOyVHAAv8oMUll4Bw3KJo1TxCWDEnil3KU/edit)). This would eliminate cross-zone data transfer costs but would not mitigate the cost increase associated with six smaller, fragmented clusters[\[25\]](#bookmark=id.tih5liz9br3y).

The ideal solution is to restrict the spark-prod-iad cluster in one single zone. Use only on-demand instances, purchase ODCR for our desirable instance types to guarantee SLA for critical workloads. This would match our plan with EMR, and we will forfeit many of the key advantages we aimed to leverage by using Kubernetes, such as cross-AZ EC2 capacity availability.

# 42% cost increase from EC2 instance family differences {#42%-cost-increase-from-ec2-instance-family-differences}

According to AWS [documentation](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/graviton/getting-started/), Graviton2-based instances deliver up to 40% better price performance compared to comparable x86-based instances, and Graviton3 processors further enhance performance, providing up to 25% better compute performance over Graviton2.

We are running **Graviton3** instances on EMR. We currently have ODCR for 400 instances of the type **r7g.16xlarge**. These instances are more than powerful enough for our usage (e.g. the CPU load is very low). The current EKS cluster uses **3rd generation AMD EPYC processors,** which is about 30% more expensive than the equivalent Graviton3 instances that provide the same amount of resources.

Currently, the 15 nodes in the **spark-prod-iad** cluster primarily use instance types that result in significant inefficiencies for Spark workloads[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.rwe72e8dlnev). This is primarily because Karpenter determined that these instance types are the best fit for long-running pods that are not Spark jobs. If the EKS cluster and Karpenter autoscaler were configured to select the most desirable instance type from the AMD EPYC processors, such as the **r7a.16xlarge**, the hourly cost would be $4.8688. In comparison, the hourly cost of the current **Graviton3 instance type r7g.16xlarge** is $3.4272. This represents a **42%** cost increase if we move our production workload from EMR. We also need to make sure EKS and Karpenter can guarantee the desirable instance types[\[21\]](#bookmark=id.6y17fp0y5an), as I believe the Karpenter’s dynamic nature can create extra cost risk if not managed carefully[\[23\]](#bookmark=id.rtp5uuguqftd). 

# 25% increase of cluster cost from significantly increased EBS cost {#25%-increase-of-cluster-cost-from-significantly-increased-ebs-cost}

Currently, we provision **200GB of disk space for Spark drivers** ([source](https://slack-github.com/slack/spark-on-k8s/blob/088a0aad78bea909b0e9780b0af2aefa86b60cb3/charts/spark-operator/templates/default-configs/spark-defaults-cm.yaml#L86)) and **100GB for Spark executors** ([source](https://slack-github.com/slack/spark-on-k8s/blob/088a0aad78bea909b0e9780b0af2aefa86b60cb3/charts/spark-operator/templates/default-configs/spark-defaults-cm.yaml#L81)) through Persistent Volume Claims (PVC).

To make a conservative estimation, let’s assume the **spark-prod-iad** cluster can acquire **r6a.16xlarge** instances for Spark workloads. These nodes have a pod capacity of 54\. Accounting for 6 system pods, we assume the remaining 48 pods are allocated to executors. With each executor pod requesting **100GB of disk space**, the required EBS volume would be at **4.8TB** per node. 

In comparison, our most expensive yet cost-efficient cluster is the **prod-data-platform-emr6** cluster. Currently, we attach only **700GB of EBS volume** for an equivalent **r7g.16xlarge** instance in the task fleet. This represents a **500% increase in cost** compared to the status quo with EMR. Even with a more conservative estimate, considering the small number of core nodes that use **1.2TB of EBS per node**, the EBS cost is still **300% higher** than the current status quo. This is based on a reasonable assumption that we do expect more than 40 containers on a single **r7g.16xlarge** node in the **prod-data-platform-emr6** cluster[\[6\]](#bookmark=id.mm4x2bliwtu1).

For the **prod-data-platform-emr6** cluster, we pay approximately $100 per day for EBS and $1500 per day for EC2[\[10\]](#bookmark=id.ixz8vlw6hynw). If EBS costs were to increase by a factor of five, the overall cluster cost would increase by approximately 25%.

However, this is a best-case scenario. Considering a more realistic workload, such as the SQL-based jobs on the **prod-dma-emr6** cluster, which are more difficult to optimize for disk usage, we pay $200 per day for EBS and $1200 per day for EC2[\[11\]](#bookmark=id.8z69ypgtvb9a). In this case, a fivefold increase in EBS costs would lead to a 50% increase in the total cluster cost.

There is no known effective mitigation for this cost increase. This is because: 

* executor PVC sizing must be based on the most skewed executor, presenting the same cost risk as memory sizing (see [Spark Tuning: Right-sizing containers](https://docs.google.com/document/d/199wkuvnvGWK5-5qdq83baCbvKaX3H_WiFwjJJXHDwY0/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit))  
* estimating such skewed disk requirements is challenging if possible. Spark uses disk for shuffling, disk spilling, and dataset/dataframe caching, and these metrics are difficult to collect reliably.

# 35% cost increase from shuffle tracking timeout {#35%-cost-increase-from-shuffle-tracking-timeout}

A significant difference between running Spark on YARN and Kubernetes is the use of the External Shuffle Service on YARN. This service allows a Spark executor to quickly decommission without waiting for the next stage to complete its shuffle with dynamic allocation enabled. It is very difficult to run External Shuffle Service on Kubernetes, so as a compromise, [SPARK-27963](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-27963) introduced the shuffle tracking feature for running Spark on Kubernetes.

The current shuffle tracking timeout ([source code link](https://slack-github.com/slack/spark-on-k8s/blob/088a0aad78bea909b0e9780b0af2aefa86b60cb3/charts/spark-operator/templates/default-configs/spark-defaults-cm.yaml#L107)) is set to **7200 seconds (2 hours)**. This means that executors holding shuffle data will be retained for 2 hours before being decommissioned during dynamic allocation. Since less than 1% of our Spark applications run longer than this timeout[\[24\]](#bookmark=id.upjvqw3ljebt), this configuration effectively disables dynamic allocation for most of our applications. Given that Spark’s Dynamic Executor Allocation (**DEA**) is a well-known cost-saving technique with an expected saving rate of 20-50% depending on the workload, we can estimate that the cluster cost will increase by 35%.

There is little room for improvement to reduce this cost increase by using shuffle tracking on Kubernetes[\[26\]](#[26]-shuffle-tracking-is-a-large-cost-risk-on-kubernetes). To mitigate this risk, we can deploy Apache Celeborn (as proposed in [Improving Spark Shuffle Reliability](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1xkddnyMmSJOyVHAAv8oMUll4Bw3KJo1TxCWDEnil3KU/edit)). 

# 150% memory overhead to avoid OOM failures {#150%-memory-overhead-to-avoid-oom-failures}

There is a fundamental difference between how YARN and Kubernetes enforce memory usages of Spark containers. On YARN, the NodeManager periodically collects resource usage metrics from containers, including memory consumption. The collected metrics are compared against the memory limits assigned to each container.  If a container's memory usage surpasses its limit, it's flagged as a potential offender. If a container is found to be consistently exceeding its memory limits, NodeManager can take action to kill the container.

In Kubernetes, a container cannot use more memory than its configured resource limit (resource.limits.memory). The memory limit is enforced by cgroups at the kernel level. If a Spark container uses more memory than the limit, the kernel’s OOM Killer will terminate the container. 

In other words, YARN's memory enforcement is less strict than Kubernetes'. To maintain the current level of reliability[\[8\]](#bookmark=id.8c3pk1pbwnuq) and accommodate the ongoing project of right-sizing Spark executors ([Spark Tuning: Right-sizing containers](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/199wkuvnvGWK5-5qdq83baCbvKaX3H_WiFwjJJXHDwY0/edit)), we may have to allocate extra memory for the same jobs when migrating from EMR. As of today, the non-heap memory is configured to be 150% that of on-heap memory, significantly higher than the default 10% overhead ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/data-airflow/pull/56680)). This will effectively increase the cost of Spark jobs by 150%.

This risk can be largely mitigated, but it will still be more expensive than EMR due to the waste created from resource fragmentation. I estimate that we could potentially reduce the cost increase to only 5%.

# 238% increase based on latest FY26 road map which reverse some key cost saving efforts on EMR {#238%-increase-based-on-latest-fy26-road-map-which-reverse-some-key-cost-saving-efforts-on-emr}

According to the recent FY26 road map for spark-on-k8s project, we will start enabling cross-AZ clusters, and also enable the usage of spot instances and autoscaling for the spark-on-k8s cluster. Based on the current state of the cluster[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.rwe72e8dlnev), it contains those most undesirable instances with a memory-to-cpu ratio as low as 2 (c6a.xlarge), which is the type of instances we removed in FY25Q1, with concrete evidence that they can immediately double the cost of the cluster (see [evidence](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1717430729675129?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A)). 

To better estimate the cost impact of this FY26 road map, we can examine the cost saving result of the reverse of the action. In FY25Q1, the cost of the data-platform clusters started with $459,595 in February, to $135,740 in May[\[27\]](#bookmark=id.y9q2h6b04993). During this period, the main contribution of this large cost savings are the following:

* We merged the 3 (or 4\) data-platform clusters into one. Fragmented smaller clusters create large waste.  
* We removed undesirable EC2 instances, the instances with undesirable memory-to-cup ratio (which we have today in the spark-prod-iad)  
* We replaced spot instances with on-demand instances, and covered some of them with ODCR. Please see [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit) 

We can say with confidence that if we were to reverse the action, split the data-platform cluster into 3 auto-scaled sub clusters, and adopt spot instances for them, the cost will increase from $135,740 to $459,595. This represents a 238% increase[\[27\]](#bookmark=id.y9q2h6b04993). 

This is a conservative estimate because we only have 3 data-platform sub clusters. The more autoscaled clusters we have, the more waste we generate. The road map for spark-on-k8s implies splitting the cluster into 6 subclusters, which will incur much higher waste. To estimate the potential cost increase from splitting the cluster into six auto-scaled sub-clusters with spot instances, we can examine Appendix [\[27\]](#bookmark=id.y9q2h6b04993) in more detail. The data reveals that merging data-platform2 and data-platform3 into data-platform eliminated the costs of the two sub-clusters while maintaining the overall cost of data-platform. This aligns with my earlier estimate of a 30% cost overhead associated with cluster splitting, prior to cost savings, and the observed nearly two-thirds cost reduction through merging 3 clusters. Applying this heuristic to a six-sub-cluster scenario suggests a potential a total of six-fold of the original cost, or up to 500%, increase in total cost.

This estimate is independent of other cost increase projections. For example, I previously estimated a 42% increase due to the transition from cheaper Graviton3 instances on EMR. This estimate only considers the cost difference between equivalent instance types with different processors and does not account for the additional waste incurred by the current Spark on Kubernetes cluster's acceptance of less desirable instance types.

# Appendix

## \[1\] Spark shuffle impact on cost

Data transferred between EC2 instances across different AZs within the same region is charged at $0.01 per GB for both inbound and outbound traffic. This means that [each GB of data transfer incurs a total of $0.02](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/on-demand/#Data_Transfer). 

The following table (see [live query](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001534717)) indicates that, with the current EKS configuration, the increased cross-AZ data transfer from Spark shuffling alone would cost approximately $8 million annually. Since we lack complete metrics for September (as data collection began mid-month) and November (at the time of writing this document), we will use the October numbers as a reference. The total EMR cost for October is **$323,883**\[2\], while the projected cost for data shuffling is **$732,699**. This means the additional cost of shuffling alone is more than double the total EMR cost.

![][image1]

## \[2\] We have saved $8.3 million annually from EMR in 2024

We currently operate a total of 35 EMR clusters, including 11 IDR clusters. Our EMR cost-saving initiative began in March 2024\. For the 24 non-IDR clusters, we reduced the total EMR cost from **$1,015,870** in February to **$323,883** in October, achieving a 68% savings rate. This translates to an annual savings of **$8.3 million**.

See AWS Cost Explorer link [here](https://us-east-1.console.aws.amazon.com/costmanagement/home?region=us-east-1#/cost-explorer?chartStyle=STACK&costAggregate=netAmortizedCost&endDate=2024-10-31&excludeForecasting=false&filter=%5B%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22CostCategoryName%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Cost%20category%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22%7D%7D,%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22TagKey%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Tag%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools3-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dea-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dea-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-search-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-search-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-infra-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-infra-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-tools-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-tools-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-insights-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-insights-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-interop-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-interop-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-sqooper-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-sqooper-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-ingestion-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-ingestion-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-percolator-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-percolator-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-security-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-security-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22adhoc-manitoba-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22adhoc-manitoba-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22backfill-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22backfill-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22dev-sqooper-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22dev-sqooper-emr6%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22displayValue%22:%22Name%22,%22value%22:%22Name%22%7D%7D%5D&futureRelativeRange=CUSTOM&granularity=Monthly&groupBy=%5B%22TagKeyValue:Name%22%5D&historicalRelativeRange=CUSTOM&isDefault=false&reportArn=arn:aws:ce::429538831549:ce-saved-report%2F1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportId=1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportName=New%20cost%20and%20usage%20report&showOnlyUncategorized=false&showOnlyUntagged=false&startDate=2024-02-01&usageAggregate=undefined&useNormalizedUnits=false).

![][image2]

## \[3\] Summary of 15 Nodes in the spark-prod-iad EKS cluster

The **spark-prod-iad** cluster currently handles minimal Spark workloads (mostly empty majority of the time) and is maintaining a baseline capacity of 15 nodes. The table below provides a snapshot of the current stats for these 15 nodes. It’s important to note that, with the planned implementation of node recycling and dynamic scaling using Karpenter, the instance types and sizes are expected to change over time.

It is apparent from this table that:

- The cluster is running EC2 nodes across all Availability Zones in the us-east-1 region  
- The cluster is currently using various instance types, all powered by AMD EPYC processors, which are about 30% more expensive than equivalent Graviton3 instances we use today.  
- The cluster is using instance families that are highly inefficient for Spark workloads. For example, the c6 instance family results in an immediate 80% waste of CPU resources (see  [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit)). Removing such undesirable instance types is the main contribution for the EMR cost saving effort. It is not clear how much of this can be done on EKS/bedrock. 

| Node | Instance Type | AZ | vCore | Memory | Notable Pods |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| [ip-10-15-248-233.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-15-248-233.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | c6a.xlarge | us-east-1a | 4 | 8GB | Looks like this Node is one of the two nodes running Karpenter Controller container, which has a resource request of cpu:2,memory:4G, and limit of cpu:4,memory:6G |
| [ip-10-23-251-110.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-23-251-110.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | c6a.xlarge | us-east-1f | 4 | 8GB | Same with above |
| [ip-10-22-252-199.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-22-252-199.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.xlarge | us-east-1c |  |  |  |
| [ip-10-15-252-77.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-15-252-77.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1a |  |  |  |
| [ip-10-15-248-84.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-15-248-84.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1a | 16 | 128 |  |
| [ip-10-21-251-233.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-21-251-233.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1b |  |  | Mostly running the spark-operators. At the moment it runs 6 operator pods. |
| [ip-10-25-248-209.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-25-248-209.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1d | 16 | 128 | Currently runs 12 spark-operator pods. A bunch of pods for argocd 2 working spark-history pods, and 2 failing/backoff spark-history pods. |
| [ip-10-21-254-230.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-21-254-230.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | c6a.xlarge | us-east-1b |  |  | Bapi2-operator-controllerKubernetes-dashboardKube-events-monitorSlack-sloop (request: 500m/9G, limit: 2/18G) |
| [ip-10-21-249-54.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-21-249-54.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | m6a.xlarge | us-east-1b |  |  | Bapi2-operator-controller-managerKube-events-monitorKubernetes-dashboard-websloop-spark-prod-iad |
| [ip-10-21-250-36.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-21-250-36.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | m6a.xlarge | us-east-1b |  |  | Metrics-serverNode-taintHpa-monitorKubernetes-dashboard-api |
| [ip-10-22-251-192.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-22-251-192.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | c6a.2xlarge | us-east-1c |  |  | Metrics-serverKubecost-prometheus-server |
| [ip-10-15-251-87.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-15-251-87.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.8xlarge | us-east-1a |  |  | Spark-operatorsExternal-secretsExternal-secrets-cert-controllerPrometheus-prometheusAdmission-webhookyunikorn-scheduler(yunikorn-scheduler-k8s:request:3/8G, yunikorn-scheduler-web:1/2G) |
| [ip-10-15-255-129.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-15-255-129.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1a |  |  | Spark-operatorYunikorn-admission-controllerCortex-k8s-agentSpark-historySpark-infra-ingress-nginx-controllerPrometheus-kube-prometheus-operator |
| [ip-10-21-251-228.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-21-251-228.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.4xlarge | us-east-1b |  |  | Spark-operators |
| [ip-10-25-248-190.ec2.internal](https://kubedash-spark-prod-iad.tinyspeck.com/#/node/ip-10-25-248-190.ec2.internal?namespace=default) | r6a.xlarge | us-east-1d |  |  | Custom-metrics-apiserverKubernetes-dashboard-metrics-scraperKubernetes-dashboard-kongKubernetes-dashboard-nginxkubecost-cost-analyzer(cost-model container request 2/16G) |

## \[4\] We run about 4000 Spark applications per day

See this query: [https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001534801](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001534801)

![][image3]

## \[5\] We run an average of 1.25 million to 1.5 million Spark executors a day

See this query: [https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001534860](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001534860)

![][image4]

## \[6\] On a steady state, the data-platform cluster runs 20-40 containers on each machine

The following screenshot shows a snapshot of the data-platform cluster nodes running r7g.16xlarge machines (Nov 28, 8am). It shows that each machine can run more than 40 containers.

![][image5]

At the same time (Nov 28, 8am), the average disk utilization is less than 88%.  
![][image6]

## \[7\] Our Spark executors have an average lifecycle of 10 minutes, with a P50 duration of 3 minutes.

This [query](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001535429) displays the average executor duration by day. On average, each Spark executor is estimated to have a lifecycle of approximately 10 minutes.

![][image7]

This [query](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001535435) shows the executor durations in percentiles. It shows that the average duration is 10.26 minutes on Nov 27, but P50 duration is 3 minutes.

![][image8]

## \[8\] Reliability of Spark on YARN/EMR

By the end of FY25Q2, we have reached a new Spark on EMR reliability milestone, that reliability is not a concern and the goal is to maintain the status quo (see [2025 Q1, Q2 EMR/Spark Reliability ](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1vEQ5UD_r-cnqmI33CBxIDprPv074OHdYe_09wiZZeMY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)). In FY25 Q3, we’ve identified the next large reliability improvement opportunity (see [Thoughts on Merlin Backfill](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/121mbhx08qjmLgo-rbYIXqroUAeyRhugwJnRHYUb4sjM/edit)). We can confidently state that we now maintain a very high reliability standard for running Spark on EMR, and we anticipate further improvements once the Merlin backfill process is optimized.

The following query and chart shows that 97% of all Spark tasks succeeded on its first try on Nov 26\.   
[https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001535444](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001535444)

**![][image9]**

## \[9\] Unknown risks of Karpenter for Spark workload

One way Karpenter can achieve cost savings is by disrupting running pods and consolidating workloads. However, this approach isn’t suitable for Spark pods, as they are stateful and rely on large Persistent Volumes (PVs). Such disruption will trigger unnecessary recomputation which adds to cost increase. 

## \[10\] EBS cost for the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster

The **prod-data-platform-emr6** cluster has the most efficient EBS usage. We attach the smallest possible EBS volumes to each instance, and the EBS size is proportional to the EC2 instance type. According to AWS Cost Explorer (using daily costs from November 26th as an example), we pay approximately $100 per day for EBS for every $1500 per day spent on EC2 instances.

![][image10]

## \[11\] EBS cost for prod-dma-emr6 cluster

We need to attach larger EBS volumes to the **prod-dma-emr6** cluster. This is because the workloads on this cluster are primarily SQL-based jobs, which are more difficult to tune. As a result, we need more disk space to ensure reliability until we can optimize the jobs to reduce unnecessary shuffle storage and disk spills.

Using the daily cost on November 26 as an example, we are paying $200 for EBS while we are paying $1176 for the EC2.

![][image11]

## \[12\] It takes more than 30 seconds to pull images for Spark pods

The following example shows it takes 32 seconds to pull the image for beagle-thresholds job for the image of  size 1309394413 bytes.![][image12]

For the other task api-sigs-expire-api, it takes almost 40 seconds for ImagePull

![][image13]

## \[13\] The reasons running Spark on Kubernetes can be more expensive than on YARN/EMR

We have saved over $8 million annually, primarily by reducing resource fragmentation. Resource fragmentation at various levels decreases the efficiency of binpacking, leading to increased waste. For EMR cost savings, we have focused on minimizing resource fragmentation across multiple levels:

* **AZ Level**: We are consolidating clusters into a specific Availability Zone (AZ) to reduce ODCR fragmentation. Since ODCR is purchased per AZ, running more clusters in the same AZ minimizes waste from unused ODCR capacity.  
* **Cluster Level**: We are merging smaller clusters into larger ones. For example, we consolidated sub-clusters such as three data-platform clusters, three DMA clusters, and three Odin-tools clusters into a single cluster. We are also in the process of combining team-based clusters.  
* **Machine Level**: We have largely eliminated small instances and now exclusively use **r7g.16xlarge** instances in our clusters. Larger machines enable the scheduler to pack more containers efficiently, reducing resource waste. Additionally, they help minimize disk space fragmentation, further decreasing waste.  
* **Application Level**: Since Q2, we have manually identified and optimized wasteful applications due to an erroneous default container sizing (1core/4GB). Slightly larger containers reduce the container-level resource fragmentation. We are now scaling this effort by providing education and directly engaging with pipeline owners.

Running Spark on Kubernetes inherently increases resource fragmentation due to the design of resource isolation and guarantees provided by cgroups. Based on today’s examination of the Spark EKS cluster, I have identified the following resource fragmentation risks:

* **Machine Level**: This cluster appears to use EC2 instances as small as **r6a.4xlarge**. Smaller instances tend to waste a larger percentage of their resources.   
* **Pod Level**: Each Spark driver and executor pod must allocate more resources (primarily memory) compared to their counterparts on YARN to prevent unnecessary OOM (Out of Memory) kills.  
* **Storage Level**: Each Spark driver and executor is attached to a PVC with a fixed size, seemingly backed by EBS storage. This setup creates pod-level storage resource fragmentation. For instance, an executor that doesn’t use any disk will waste 100 GiB of PVC storage, which cannot be utilized by other pods on the same node. As of today, I estimate that the cost of EBS is at least 3 times larger than that on the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster (and we can continue to reduce EBS cost for this cluster).   
* **AZ Level**: Shuffle is unavoidable. With the EKS cluster running nodes across multiple AZs, I estimate this will increase costs by at least $3 million per year—a cost we currently do not incur with EMR.  
* **Pod Level**: Each Spark driver pod includes sidecars. For example, each driver pod runs a Nebula container, adding a minimum memory overhead of 128 MiB. For an **r6a.16xlarge** instance, this translates to a minimum memory overhead of 6 GiB.  
* **EC2 Instance Level**: It appears the cluster is using the **r6a** instance family, which is over 30% more expensive than the **r6g** family. Additionally, **r7g** instances are at least 10% more cost-efficient than **r6g**. As a result, from an EC2 instance cost perspective, running Spark on EKS is 40% more expensive than on EMR today. This is unlikely to change in the short term, as EMR is currently the only platform within Slack utilizing the more cost-effective Graviton instance types. Moving from EMR to EKS will incur this instance cost increase. 

## \[14\] Current Spark workload in the spark-prod-iad cluster

| Pod  | app\_name | dag | task | PVC | Resource Requests and Limits |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| beagle-thresholds-us-airflow-prod | Beagle-threasholds-us-airflow-prod-xxxxxSpark-exec-id:37 Spark-role:executorspark-version:3.3.3 | beagle\_thresholds\_user\_downloads | user\_downloads\_agg | 100G for executor, 200G for driver | Cpu:1, memory:5734Mi for driverCpu:2, memory:11000Mi for executor |
| api\_sigs |  | api\_sigs | expire\_api\_calls\_with\_params |  | Cpu:1, memory:5734Mi for driver containerNebula-cni-init: cpu:100m, memory:128Mi |

How do we use PVC?  
Looks like SPARK\_LOCAL\_DIRS is configured to /opt/spark/local-dir, which is mounted to a PVC. 

## \[15\] The need to synchronize the Kubernetes resource request with 

Spark executor memory of this executor is set to 4400m with SPARK\_EXECUTOR\_MEMORY environment variable, although this executor pod has a memory request and limit of 11000 Mi.

Note: this is because of the 150% overhead default setting. See [https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1733242957813589](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1733242957813589)

![][image14]

## \[16\] Potential risk of increased cost for driver pod not cleaned up in time

The following screenshot shows a 200G PVC still in bound state 6 days after it has been created, caused by the delayed garbage collection of driver pods in completed state. It appears that the driver pod for this task was kept alive for 6 days assuming this is a daily job. 

![][image15]

The following is a list of current PVC in bound state that are more than a day old. It appears that the driver pod was kept for 6 days.

![][image16]

## \[17\] Kubernetes introduces performance overhead and risks

* Docker uses an overlay file system, which incurs performance overhead when reading and writing large files. Disk IO is the main performance bottleneck for Spark workloads (including shuffling, disk spills, disk-based persistence of Spark datasets/dataframes).  
* Long ImagePull (30+ seconds) add additional delay and reliably risks for a job that is already running  
* Docker introduces a network abstraction layer to manage container networking, which add additional overhead to network traffic. In particular, Docker’s default bridge network, which uses network address translation (NAT), can introduce latency especially for outbound traffic.   
* The container runtime, such as containerd, adds a layer of abstraction between the container and the host network. This can introduce additional processing overhead, which may impact network latency.

## \[18\] The reliability risks of a 10-minute shuffle tracking timeout

Today, it takes about 10 minutes from when a new EC2 instance is launched to when it is ready to accept workload. The expectation of Karpenter is also in the range of a few minutes. On EKS, there is a risk for long delay in starting up new executors due to additional overhead from pulling images for all overhead pods (e.g. `twistlock-defender`, `aws-node`, `calico-node`, `kube-proxy`, and `ebs-csi-node`). Additionally, launching executor pods on newly provisioned nodes can take time with additional delay. For instance, pulling the specific Spark application image on a clean node can take at least 30 seconds[\[12\]](#bookmark=id.4zal9db8klpu). This delay is expected to be significantly longer in production environments, especially when pulling images for 40+ pods (if we implement node anti-affinity) can hit multiple bottlenecks in the system. Having a 10-minute timeout is highly risky for critical workload if we do not have ODCR and encounter insufficient capacity.

Consider the same example with data skew mentioned above, with a job with large data skew, that 1 executor runs for one hour, and the remaining 999 executors run just a few minutes. After 10 minutes, the 999 executors would be decommissioned, removing all their shuffle files. If the straggler executor completes its task after running for an hour, the driver would need to recompute all the lost shuffle files from those 999 executors, triggering a stage-level retry.

In the best-case scenario, this recomputation would occur only once (though it is still unnecessary). However, in practice, the cost risk is much higher. If a single pod out of the 999 nodes is delayed and cannot finish within 10 minutes (for reasons including the delay for spinning up a new machine, image-pull all containers, Insufficient Capacity Error for EC2 etc), the dynamic allocation would have removed the skewed executor and we will lose the shuffle file that took an hour to compute, and we will need to re-compute that skewed task, which takes another hour. By the time that skewed task finishes, all other shuffle files would be gone and need another re-compute. Every such retry increases the cost by 100% until the job fails after exhausting its retry limit.

## \[19\] The bursty, batch nature of Spark jobs introduces cost risks if the cluster becomes fragmented

Kubernetes is ideally suited for long-running, stateless services that rely on external storage backends, such as databases. In contrast, Spark is primarily a batch-processing compute engine. In our Spark infrastructure, many workloads require a large number of executors.

As demonstrated [here](https://hound.tinyspeck.com/?q=spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors&i=nope&literal=nope&files=&excludeFiles=&repos=), numerous Spark applications are designed to spin up a significant number of executors during their runtime. For example, **flog\_by\_api**, a critical job owned by the data ingestion team, runs once a day and requires up to [3,000 executors](https://slack-github.com/slack/data-airflow/blob/27033b49363063266f2c5a6a2a6dae59139439d7/dags/data_ingestion/flog_by_api/dag.py#L176) for optimal performance.

Currently, to the best of my knowledge, the highest-capacity instance available in the **spark-prod-iad** cluster is **r6a.16xlarge**, which supports up to 48 pods per instance. This means that once a day, this Spark job would request approximately 60 additional machines of this type (assuming Karpenter can make such a scaling decision).

From our experience, requesting 60 instances simultaneously poses significant risks, particularly for the most desirable instance types. In the past couple of months, we have frequently escalated issues caused by a lack of EC2 capacity from AWS. For example, in one instance, a cluster configured with autoscaling was unable to obtain more than 10 machines for multiple hours due to the unavailability of both spot and on-demand instances in the entire zone (see in [\#escal-data](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1731346545478079?thread_ts=1730978274.801519&cid=C01FB307Z7A)). If we want to protect our critical jobs with SLA on EKS, we will need to over-purchase ODCR for such instances in every zone, which will generate significant waste from unutilized ODCR.

## \[20\] We cannot use spot instances for Spark workload

Spark jobs are stateful, and spot interruptions can lead to significant reliability issues and increased costs due to retries. Additionally, for those desirable EC2 instances, spot instances provide a maximum discount of 50%. The same discount can be achieved by using a Savings Plan for on-demand instances. This means that spot instances offer no cost advantage for Spark workloads but come with significant risks.

In fact, replacing spot instances with on-demand instances played a major role in our cost-saving efforts on EMR in 2024\. Please see detailed explanation in [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit)

## \[21\] We have limited options for desirable EC2 instance types

Spark jobs have a high memory-to-CPU ratio, limiting us to selecting EC2 instances that offer a similar ratio. Additionally, we prefer larger instances to minimize resource fragmentation and reduce waste. Currently, our most desirable EC2 instances are **r7g.16xlarge** and all **r8g** family instances larger than **16xlarge**. The full analysis is documented here in [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit). As a matter of fact, we did see the cost double when in one instance we allowed the cluster to use those undesirable instance types (see [\#escal-data](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1717430729675129?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A)).

![][image17]

Our EMR clusters used to accept any instance type (similar to what the spark-prod-iad today). Removing undesirable instances and small instances have contributed to the large cost savings from EMR this year[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.1erac0qo5pxq). 

## \[22\] Autoscaling Spark clusters is both costly and poses a reliability risk

Contrary to the common belief that autoscaling is beneficial and cost-saving, autoscaling Spark clusters, especially small clusters, is both risky and expensive. Please see the risks documented [here](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C8EDM9MGA/p1726601113255819). Spark can benefit from an autoscaled cluster, but it's limited to a few use cases that require careful engineering effort to take advantage of without uncontrolled cost increase.

## \[23\] Risks for using Karpenter for Spark workloads

Dependency on spot instances is one of Karpenter’s benefits, which we cannot take advantage of[\[20\]](#bookmark=id.n2do5jd8cfuq). Karpenter offers fast autoscaling, which is also wasteful and risky for Spark jobs[\[22\]](#bookmark=id.qippfqyr1vgz). 

Karpenter can select instance types based on workload demands, which can work against us in terms of cost due to unpredictable resource needs over time[19\]](#bookmark=id.l8k6obrk60q9). Without restricting instance types, Karpenter may choose smaller instances, leading to unnecessary resource fragmentation and waste.

Karpenter’s node consolidation can also work against us for the same reason. Disrupting a long-running executor only to recompute it on a different machine introduces unnecessary risk. For Spark workloads, it is best to completely disable node consolidation and all autoscaling features.

Spark’s batch-oriented nature conflicts with Karpenter’s optimization for dynamic, long-running workloads. Short-lived Spark jobs[\[7\]](#bookmark=id.e01raid50zj3) may not fully utilize newly provisioned nodes, leading to cost inefficiencies. 

## \[24\] More than 90% of all Spark applications finishes within 1500 seconds

Using this [query](https://analytics.tinyspeck.com/v3/explore/1000000001536011), using a peak day on November 27 as an example: 99% of all Spark applications finished within 7,816 seconds, 98% within 4,650 seconds, 90% within 1,473 seconds, and 75% within 445 seconds. Half of the applications finished within 128 seconds.

![][image18]

## \[25\] Multiple autoscaled clusters are very wasteful

It’s an industry best practice that running a small number of large clusters is generally more cost-effective than a large number of smaller clusters. Larger clusters utilize resources more efficiently, reducing fragmentation. This principle applies to both EKS and EMR. This is why large companies tend to run a smaller number of large clusters. For example, LinkedIn’s Spark cluster comprises 15,000 machines, forming a single logical cluster across two physical YARN clusters.

The root cause of large waste from running small, autoscaled clusters are mainly the following:

* Low resource utilization ratio during scaling up and scaling down  
* Much more difficult to cap the capacity of large number of small clusters  
* Independent scaling among small clusters forces the usage of spot instances, which does not have price benefit compared to on-demand instances under Savings Plan, but they create large waste through forced recomputation.

To provide a concrete EKS example, while Karpenter can provision new nodes for Slack in about 4 minutes, subsequent image pulls for each container can take over 30 seconds[\[12\]](#bookmark=id.4zal9db8klpu). Considering our Spark executors’ median lifespan of 3 minutes[\[7\]](#bookmark=id.e01raid50zj3), it means a 16% overhead just for image pulling. 

This is a conservative estimate, as the 30-second image pull duration is for a very small image in a nearly empty cluster. Image pulling is a known risk when running Spark on Kubernetes. Reliability challenges can arise when a security patch to a base image triggers large-scale cache misses, causing all containers to simultaneously pull images.

At decommission time, nodes cannot be decommissioned until all Pods can be decommissioned. If one executor Pod has a long shuffle tracking timeout and must remain alive after the job finishes, the node must also remain active. Since long timeouts are necessary for reliability, node decommission can be wastefull[\[18\]](#bookmark=id.2w25hau6d90d). 

We’ve achieved significant cost savings by consolidating clusters. To illustrate the potential cost increase of dividing clusters across multiple Availability Zones, we can highlight the savings realized from combining smaller clusters. Previously, we ran multiple “sub” clusters, dividing a single logical cluster into three smaller subclusters and using a round-robin router to distribute workloads. Each subcluster scaled independently and could run in different Availability Zones.

Before May 7, 2024, we ran three small, autoscaled clusters for **odin-tools**, all using spot instances. We consolidated these three clusters into a single cluster, continuing to use spot instances. The following daily cost chart from AWS Cost Explorer demonstrates that merging two of the clusters resulted in savings equivalent to the total cost of those merged clusters.

![][image19]

We achieved similar results by consolidating all three **data-platform** clusters into a single cluster. The key difference is that these three clusters were all auto-scaled and used on-demand instances instead of spot instances. The following Cost Explorer chart ([link](https://us-east-1.console.aws.amazon.com/costmanagement/home?region=us-east-1#/cost-explorer?chartStyle=STACK&costAggregate=netAmortizedCost&endDate=2024-03-31&excludeForecasting=false&filter=%5B%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22CostCategoryName%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Cost%20category%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22%7D%7D,%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22TagKey%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Tag%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Name%22%7D%7D%5D&futureRelativeRange=CUSTOM&granularity=Daily&groupBy=%5B%22TagKeyValue:Name%22%5D&historicalRelativeRange=CUSTOM&isDefault=false&reportArn=arn:aws:ce::122735864621:ce-saved-report%2F917c2af4-d19c-4f95-8345-8269e68cb11a&reportId=917c2af4-d19c-4f95-8345-8269e68cb11a&reportName=EMR%2012%20Cluster%20Cost%20Report&showOnlyUncategorized=false&showOnlyUntagged=false&startDate=2024-03-01&usageAggregate=undefined&useNormalizedUnits=false)) shows how one large cluster fully absorbed the cost of two other clusters. 

![][image20]

## \[26\] Shuffle tracking is a large cost risk on Kubernetes {#[26]-shuffle-tracking-is-a-large-cost-risk-on-kubernetes}

This estimation of 35% increase in cost after moving from EMR to KES is conservative because a significant portion of our Spark workloads, such as Merlin jobs, have large data skew, and those jobs requires longer shuffle tracking timeout to maintain the existing reliability status quo[\[8\]](#bookmark=id.8c3pk1pbwnuq) and reduce the risk of increased costs due to recomputation. Setting 2-hour shuffle tracking will increase the cost of these jobs significantly. To illustrate this risk with a concrete example, consider a scenario where a job requires 1,000 executors. One skewed executor takes an hour to complete, while the remaining 999 finish within a minute. With this configuration, all 999 executors would be kept alive for the entire hour, potentially increasing this job’s cost by up to 1,000 times.

**What about reducing the shuffle tracking timeout from 2 hours to 10 minutes?** 10 minutes is too short because it creates both reliability and cost risks[\[18\]](#bookmark=id.2w25hau6d90d). For the sake of argument, let’s assume the shuffle tracking timeout can be reduced to 10 minutes and analyze the cost impact.

We currently run up to 1.25 million containers per day[\[5\]](#bookmark=id.td4ucpawoqje). With our current Dynamic Executor Allocation configuration, executors are quickly deallocated with a default timeout of 1 minute (with a plan to reduce to 30 seconds for static clusters), resulting in an average executor lifespan duration of only 10 minutes[\[7\]](#bookmark=id.e01raid50zj3). Setting a 10-minute shuffle tracking timeout would effectively double the average executor lifespan, leading to a doubling of costs (both EC2 cost and EBS cost). 

We will also unfairly penalize better-tuned Spark jobs with shorter executor lifespans. This is because optimized jobs typically result in shorter executor lifespans. Currently, the P50 executor lifespan is only 3 minutes. With a 10-minute shuffle tracking timeout, the cost attributed to half of our Spark jobs would quadruple, undermining job owners’ motivation to optimize jobs for cost efficiency.

**What about reducing the shuffle tracking timeout from 2 hours to 1 minutes to match current EMR configuration?** 

As a thought experiment, let’s assume the EKS cluster is configured similarly to EMR: no autoscaling, all fixed nodes covered under ODCR, eliminating the need to start new pods on new machines, and no 30+ second delay from ImagePull (which would require significant engineering effort to pre-pull all images across all nodes). Could we match the cost and reliability of EMR under these conditions? The answer is no.

This configuration would still cause the vast majority of our Spark jobs to fail. With a 1-minute timeout, executors would retain shuffle files for only one minute. This would require all executors for the same stage to complete within a one-minute window, the cluster to have sufficient resources available to start all executors for the next stages, and all executors to begin fetching shuffle files within the same minute. Such a scenario is highly impractical, and there is no effective mitigation for the resulting risk of cost increases or reliability degradation.

## \[27\] Cost Savings of data-platform clusters in FY25Q1

In FY25Q1, the cost of the data-platform clusters started with $459,595 in February, to $135,740 in May. 

The following cost explorer chart illustrates the impact of merging data-platform clusters and removing spot instances. After removing spot instances, and some undesirable instance types (in March), and merging them all into one cluster by April, it appears that after merging data-platform2 and data-platform3 into data-platform, the latter absorbed the workloads of the former without incurring a significant cost increase.

![][image21]
